data.title: Super Micro Computer: Despite Risks, Big Upside Ahead
data.description:
Artificial Intelligence (“AI”) has been hot. From semiconductors, to software and social media end users, and especially data center infrastructure and energy demand, we have seen stock prices soaring (see table below). However, one name that stands out for both massive AI growth and an extremely low valuation, is Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI).

Obviously, SMCI has warts on it (e.g., their auditor resigned, they’ve been dropped from the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) and volatility has been extraordinary). However, SMCI has remained a critical part of the AI megatrend, and CEO Charles Liang believes 2025 could be a banner year (thanks, in large part, to the accelerating shift from Hopper to Blackwell GPUs). After reviewing the AI landscape and the SMCI particulars, I conclude with my strong opinion on investing.

About Super Micro Computer:

Founded by current CEO, Charles Liang, in 1993, Super Micro is headquartered in Silicon Valley, just 10 miles away from its key business partner and industry leader NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) (also founded in 1993).

Super Micro makes server and digital storage solutions, necessary components in the AI value chain, and works extensively with Nvidia (Liang and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang are long-time friends).

For example, Liang recently explained:

“Driven by sustained AI demand from both existing and new customers, our growth trajectory for fiscal year 2025 remains promising. Highlighted by the beginning of our transition from [Nvidia] Hopper to Blackwell GPUs, we expect the growth in new generation platforms to accelerate as supply ramps this quarter and beyond. We have confidence that our calendar year 2025 growth could be a repeat of calendar year 2023 if not better, assuming the supply chain can keep pace with demand.”

Worth mentioning, Super Micro has positioned itself as the innovative leader in liquid cooling technologies, which are necessary in data centers as AI consumes increasing power and generates substantially more heat that needs to be displaced (see the following graph for perspective on growing AI energy consumption—it’s a megatrend!).

Why Super Micro? Why Now?

As mentioned, Super Micro is a key component of the AI megatrend supply chain, for example, helping with the transition from Hopper to Blackwell GPUs, as Liang explained on the latest quarterly call:

“We are excited to announce that our Nvidia Blackwell products are shipping now. We have begun volume shipments of both air-cooled 10U and liquid-cooled 4U Nvidia B200 HGX systems. Meanwhile our Nvidia GB200 NVL72 racks are fully ready as well. Utilizing our system building blocks, we are going to soon offer more brand new platforms for customers seeking further optimized, higher density and even greener AI solutions. While most key component are ramping at full speed, it will take some time to fulfill our current AI solution backlogs.”

And considering its long relationship of partnering with Nvidia, SMCI is clearly positioned to continue benefiting dramatically from the AI megatrend. For example, Wall Street analysts now expect 60% profitable sales growth this year and another 39% next year (see table below). Further still, the AI megatrend is still in its early innings, as you can see in the following Statista chart.

The 5 Fingers of AI:

The following table includes individual stocks, each representing at least one of the five fingers of AI, such as chipmakers NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), software Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), social media Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), data center components Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT) and sharply increasing energy demands Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG).

Also shown above are Apple Inc. (AAPL), Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Vistra Corp. (VST), Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), (GOOGL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT).

And as you can see, many of these stocks have been experiencing extraordinary growth and share price gains, except for Super Micro—whose shares are still dramatically lower than 52-week highs (despite dramatic year-to-date share price momentum).

Super Micro’s Valuation:

For starters, Super Micro is profitable (healthy gross and net margins, see table above), is growing rapidly (outsized revenue growth, as mentioned) and trades at an unusually low valuation as compared to other AI stocks in the earlier table.

For example, Super Micro only trades at ~16x forward earnings and only a 0.1x forward PEG (price-to-earnings / growth). These metrics make Super Micro shares extraordinarily attractive versus the other AI megatrend opportunities, especially considering the catalysts that are ramping now (e.g., Blackwell, liquid cooling, AI megatrend, and new auditor as explained later in this report).

Super Micro’s Share Price:

Why is SMCI so inexpensive?

As illustrated by the chart above, Super Micro shares have been significantly volatile and currently sit well below recent highs, even though the revenue has continued to grow (see below). The reason is the auditor drama, delisting worries and very high short-interest (i.e., people betting against the shares).

Regarding the auditor, Ernst & Young (a highly regarded “Big-4” auditor) resigned in October, explaining:

"due to information that has recently come to our attention which has led us to no longer be able to rely on management's and the Audit Committee’s representations." EY added it is "unwilling to be associated with the financial statements prepared by management."

SMCI disagreed with E&Y, but the headline damage had already been done, and the share price plummeted. Additionally, SMCI has been working with its new auditor, BDO USA, to get its financials fully audited and filed.

Regarding delisting worries, SMCI has been given an extension to file its audited financials (following E&Y’s departure) to avoid being delisted by Nasdaq. The shares were already dropped from the exchange’s Nasdaq 100 Index (after being included for only 5 short months), and audited financials are now due by Tuesday, February 25th.

And regarding short interest, SMCI has been one of the most shorted stocks recently as many investors bet against the shares after its previous meteoric rise (more than 20% of the shares were recently sold short, see earlier table). Super Micro has also been the target of a high-profile short report by now disbanded Hindenburg Research.

On a positive note, the beat-up share price (and high short interest) create an excellent contrarian opportunity from a valuation standpoint (and from a potential short-squeeze / covering standpoint, especially ahead of the upcoming financial statement reporting extended deadline).

SMCI Risk Considerations:

From a risk management standpoint, there are various things investors may want to consider.

  • Index and Portfolio Weighing: Super Micro is currently only about 0.06% of the bellwether S&P 500 index, so it is not a big constituent at all, and investors may want to keep that in mind when weighting the shares in their own portfolios. Unlike Apple, Nvidia and Microsoft, which are all more than 5% of the index and a big part of the economy, Super Micro is less significant and investors should remember that before betting the farm. From a position sizing standpoint, anything more than a few percent of your portfolio invested in Super Micro seems very extreme and perhaps overly risky. A prudently concentrated portfolio of top ideas continues to be a winning strategy.

  • Industry Cyclicality: the semiconductor server industry is highly cyclical, and it has been very strong recently courtesy of the AI megatrend. Some argue this is a significant risk factor now, but it may also be an opportunity as AI has long-term legs, the transition from Nvidia Hopper to Blackwell is ramping, and the valuation is very low.

  • Delisting Risk: As mentioned, investors are concerned if SMCI doesn’t meet the February 25th deadline to submit its audited financial statements, it may be delisted from the Nasdaq exchange. However, as it appears short-covering could accelerate, any positive news on the financial statements could send the shares soaring even higher (they are still very inexpensive from a valuation standpoint and well below previous highs despite improving “unaudited” financials).

  • Customer Concentration: The biggest users of AI (and therefore the servers behind AI) are basically big tech (e.g., Microsoft, Facebook, Amazon, Google). And considering they’ve been spending so heavily on capex, some argue SMCI could be closer to the top of the current cycle than the bottom. However, given CEO Charles Liang’s comments and the upcoming ramp of Nvidia’s Blackwell, these concerns may be overblown (especially considering SMCI’s lead in liquid cooling), plus some estimates of continuing capex spend (see below).

Conclusion:

Super Micro shares are compelling heading into this year’s Big-Tech-capex and Blackwell ramps, especially considering liquid cooling, the extremely low valuation, building momentum, short-covering, upcoming audited financials (hopefully soon) and because other AI plays are relatively so much more expensive.

Super Micro Computer has warts, but the best opportunities often do, and the shares are worth considering for a spot in your prudently diversified, long-term, growth-focused investment portfolio.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb Stock: A Strong Buy Amid Oncology Success
data.description:
On February 6, Bristol-Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) released financial results for the fourth quarter of 2024, which, as in the last three quarters, beat Wall Street analysts' expectations with wide margins.

In this article, you, dear Seeking Alpha readers, will not only learn about the performance of its oncology and cardiovascular franchises, the achievements in developing its rich portfolio of product candidates after quite high M&A activity in the last two years, the progress of Cobenfy, which has the potential to become the "gold standard" in the schizophrenia market, especially after the failure of AbbVie's emraclidine (ABBV), but also other factors that explain why I am upgrading the Bristol-Myers Squibb rating from 'Buy' to 'Strong Buy.'

Despite falling sales of Sprycel and Revlimid, Bristol-Myers Squibb again beat the consensus estimates by a substantial margin

Bristol-Myers Squibb's revenue was about $12.34 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024, up 7.5% year-on-year and 3.8% quarter-on-quarter, driven by strong sales of various medications in its oncology and cardiovascular franchises.

So, I single out Yervoy, Opdualag, Opdivo, and Breyanzi from its oncology franchise.

In my estimation, these four drugs were the main contributors to beating my expectations and will also serve as the "foundation" of Bristol-Myers Squibb's free cash flow growth in the coming years and, as a result, will allow its management to increase its dividend payments beyond 2025.

Let's continue.

Who is the flagship of Bristol-Myers Squibb's cardiovascular franchise?

It is Camzyos (mavacamten).

As I noted in "Bristol-Myers Squibb: A Potential Comeback In Healthcare," it is a reversible cardiac myosin inhibitor and is currently only approved for one indication. However, thanks to Camzyos's unique mechanism of action and, more importantly, its excellent clinical data, I believe its label will continue to expand.

Its sales were $223 million in the fourth quarter of 2024, up 153.4% ​​year-over-year and 42.9% quarter-on-quarter, driven by increased demand in the U.S. and internationally, as well as additional data from the EXPLORER-HCM Phase 3 study, which included evidence that patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy experienced steady improvement in echocardiographic parameters.

Looking a little ahead and anticipating your questions, dear Seeking Alpha readers, now and also in the "risks" section, I will point out why Bristol-Myers Squibb's share price still does not reflect the success in commercializing its drugs.

Even though the Princeton, New Jersey-based company was able to beat the consensus forecasts again with a wide margin, namely this time by $780 million, I want to highlight the dark spots in its portfolio of FDA-approved medications are Revlimid (lenalidomide) and Sprycel (dasatinib), which are widely used to treat various types of blood cancer.

The total sales of the two blockbusters were about $1.54 billion for the three months ended December 31, 2024, down 22.2% year-on-year and 9.7% quarter-on-quarter due to Apotex's launch of a generic version of Sprycel in early September last year, and as I mentioned in "Bristol-Myers Squibb: Buy This Bargain Before It's Gone," demand for Revlimid continues to decline slightly due to increased competition from generic manufacturers including Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA), Dr. Reddy's Laboratories (RDY), Sun Pharma, and Viatris (VTRS).

On the other hand, despite the continuing pessimism from some Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha readers that I see in the comments under my previous articles, caused in part by the decline in sales of these medications, as well as the upcoming loss of Opdivo's exclusivity in 2028, the New Jersey-based company, led by its CEO Chris Boerner, has done a terrific job in recent quarters.

Although I believe that these concerns are partly justified, they are gradually losing their relevance, including due to Bristol-Myers Squibb's aggressive R&D policy, as well as the acquisition of Karuna Therapeutics, RayzeBio, and Turning Point Therapeutics, which allowed it to strengthen and rejuvenate its portfolio of drugs aimed at treating cancer and neurological disorders.

Overall, Bristol-Myers Squibb's R&D expenses remain around the $10 billion mark per year, up more than 60% from 2019, when it announced its acquisition of Celgene.

As a result, some of you may have a logical question.

If, amid increased investment in late-stage clinical development programs, Bristol-Myers Squibb was able to beat my and analysts' expectations?

The answer is yes.

So, its non-GAAP EPS was $1.67 for the three months ended December 31, 2024, beating the estimates by 20 cents, or 13.8%.

Meanwhile, Wall Street analysts forecast it will be between $1.35 and $1.64 in the first quarter of 2025, thereby implying significant year-on-year growth. By comparison, I expect it to be $1.62, which is above the midpoint of that range, in part due to stronger sales of Camzyos and Reblozyl.

BMY's strong oncology and autoimmune franchises

In my assessment, Bristol-Myers Squibb has a rich pipeline of experimental drugs, and its already FDA-approved medications have the high potential to accelerate the pace of their label expansions.

From the list of its product candidates, I would highlight arlo-cel, milvexian, golcadomide and mezigdomide, and from the recently launched medications, Sotyktu and Cobenfy.

In my opinion, they will be able to, firstly, offset the damage that will be caused by the loss of exclusivity of Eliquis and Opdivo, secondly, contribute to the revision of Wall Street analysts' forecasts for Bristol-Myers Squibb's revenue and EPS upward, and thirdly, will have a key influence in returning the interest of retail and institutional investors.

Let's go deeper.

So, Bristol-Myers Squibb is also developing arlocabtagene autoleucel [arlo-cel] to strengthen its position in the multiple myeloma therapeutics market. It is a GPRC5D-targeted CAR T-cell therapy, which, in my opinion, has a high chance of becoming the "gold standard" in this market.

Why?

On December 9, 2024, a New Jersey-based company published results from a Phase 1 study in which patients taking arlo-cel had an ORR of 87%. For those dear Seeking Alpha readers who are not familiar with the term, ORR is the percentage of patients who had a partial response [PR, 30% tumor reduction relative to baseline] or complete response [CR, meaning there were no signs of cancer in the body] in a given study, relative to the total number of study participants.

Additionally, after 18 months of follow-up, arlo-cel demonstrated a promising safety profile when compared to other FDA-approved CAR T-cell therapies, including Johnson & Johnson/Legend Biotech's Carvykti (JNJ) (LEGN) and Abecma.

I understand that this article is about Bristol-Myers Squibb, not its partner 2seventy bio (TSVT), but I want to point out that given Abecma's significantly lower efficacy, as well as its less acceptable safety profile than arlo-cel, I believe that Bristol-Myers Squibb will bet on it. In other words, 2seventy bio investors or those who consider it a long-term investment should take this into account and be extremely careful.

Also, I cannot avoid discussing the performance of Cobenfy, its recently approved medication for the treatment of schizophrenia. Sales of this M1/M4 muscarinic agonist were $10 million in Q4 2024, beating my expectations by $2 million.

In addition to the continued growth in demand for it in 2025, which Bristol-Myers Squibb noted in its presentation, another factor that makes me confident that it will become a commercially successful drug is its active development for the treatment of patients with other brain disorders.

So, on the Q4 earnings call, Christopher Boerner, stated the following.

The U.S. approval of Cobenfy in schizophrenia was an important achievement in 2024 and the launch is off to a great start. While we're focused on delivering on the schizophrenia indication today, we see the potential for additional benefit to patients and have made strategic investments in a broad clinical development program. We expect to have important data readouts starting this year and every year thereafter for the remainder of the decade.

This year, we're initiating seven Phase 3 studies across three indications, Alzheimer's disease agitation, Alzheimer's disease cognition, and bipolar 1 disorder. And next year, we plan to begin Phase 3 studies in autism spectrum disorder irritability. The significant ramp up in spending on Cobenfy illustrates our focus on continuing to invest behind key growth drivers, while simultaneously maintaining financial discipline.

Risks

It's time to discuss the key risks that, in my opinion, every Seeking Alpha reader should take into account since they are the ones that could potentially negatively affect Bristol-Myers Squibb's share price as well as its balance sheet.

Takeaway

In this article, in addition to my analysis of Bristol-Myers Squibb's financial results for the fourth quarter of 2024 and the successes and failures that have been achieved in the development of its experimental drugs, I presented my expectations regarding its prospects for 2025 and beyond.

Besides its high dividend yield of 4.51% and the fact that its net debt has decreased by over $7 billion over the last 9 months [at the end of March 2024, it was $47.4 billion, and at the end of December, $40.08 billion], another equally important factor that makes me believe Bristol-Myers Squibb is an attractive stock for investors is that it trades at multiples that are lower than those of its key competitors in the cancer therapeutics market, including AbbVie, AstraZeneca (AZN) and Novartis (NVS).

I expect its non-GAAP EPS to rise to $6.8, which corresponds to a P/E ratio of 8.14x in 2029. As a result, this figure is 56% lower than the sector median, indicating that Bristol-Myers Squibb is trading at a discount.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oatly's Oatmilk Bubble: Why The Once-Hot Brand May Be Fizzling Out
data.description:
Oatly (NASDAQ:OTLY) stock has taken a hammering in the stock market over the past year, plummeting almost 65%. The past six and nine months have been equally deplorable, with it losing north of 50% in value. Also, the recent 1-for-20 ADR ratio adjustment was another setback, sending the stock into free fall. The stock’s now flirting with its 52-week lows at $6, in what's been a forgettable year. Following the massive plunge though, bargain hunters might view it as a steal, fueling speculative tendencies. However, in taking a more holistic approach, we see a company operating in an industry facing secular decline. Hence, for those with a long-term investing horizon, Oatly stock isn't the place to hitch your ride.

Oatly Group At A Glance

Oatly Group AB was an early mover in the plant-based dairy space, specializing in oat-based products. From its humble beginnings in Sweden back in 1994, Oatly has become one of the global leaders in oat-based products including oat milk, yogurts (“oatgurts”), ice creams, spreads, and much more.

From the chart above, you can see how globally diversified the business has become. The U.S. is the company’s single largest market, which accounts for roughly 31.5% of its operating sales of $247 million. The next two slices of the pie belong to The U.K. at 17.2% ($135 million) and China at 15.3% ($119.5 million). Next in line is Germany, contributing roughly 13.4% of sales or $104.9 million, followed by Sweden at 6%. The Netherlands and Finland collectively account for over 5.5%.

Oatly’s founder, Rickard Öste, is widely regarded as the creator of oat milk, and since then, the company has popularized the niche with its innovative production and cheeky branding. Being one of the few companies in its relatively nascent industry at the time, Oatly quickly established a powerful presence before the oat milk trend went mainstream.

The pandemic was a major catalyst for the business, a time when consumers became more health-conscious, while the supply chain disruptions further reinforced the narrative. On top of that, Oatly was able to leverage its distinctive branding and powerful market presence to capitalize on the surging interest.

To put things in perspective, a pandemic-era article from Food Dive confirmed the spike in interest, citing Nielsen’s data for the 31 weeks ending October 3, 2020. The data showed that oat milk was the fastest-growing grocery category, showing a massive 212% jump in sales compared to the prior-year period. Surprisingly, this was even higher than yeast (+156%), meat alternatives (+129%), and crab (+82%). Moreover, a look at Oatly’s own financials reflects the boom, where between 2019 and 2020, the company sales more than doubled (from roughly $204 million to $421 million).

Oat Milk’s Shaky Future

The once-booming oat milk market is now under duress, amid weaker demand, stiff competition from traditional dairy, and controversial new health claims. Naturally, leading companies in the industry like Oatly have been grappling with eroding sales over the past several quarters, along with thin liquidity.

According to a recent The Week report, dairy milk is making a comeback, with a 2% increase in U.S.-based sales last year to $17.1 billion. Similarly, whole milk consumption has risen 3.2%, while plant-based milk sales fell 5.9%. Additionally, the raw milk segment surged 17.6%. Remarkably, the 3.2% rise in milk consumption, is only the second bump since the 1970s, reversing a decade-long decline. Analysts suggest the shift in trends has a lot to do with the higher price points for plant-based milk, and their “ultraprocessed” ingredient lists. Also, according to SPINS and MULO data reported by FoodNavigator, sales of plant-based milk dropped 4.7% YOY through late December.

Moreover, leading food activist and podcaster Courtney Swan recently argued that contrary to popular belief, oat milk is neither healthy nor eco-friendly. She states that oat milk tends to cause rapid blood sugar spikes, potentially leading to insulin resistance. On top of that, she also sounded the alarm over the use of glyphosate, a herbicide commonly sprayed on oat crops. Beyond the chemical aspect, she also critiqued the use of monoculture farming for growing oats, arguing that it depletes soil health and contributes to climate change.

Discussing Oatly’s Financials and Valuation

Oatly recently released its mixed Q4 2024 results, with some signs of progress, but mostly more of the same. Revenues increased 5% YOY (4.7% on a constant-currency basis) to $214.3 million, but missed estimates by a sizeable $3.81 million. Similarly, its Q4 GAAP EPS of a -$0.15 per share came in worse than expected, missing estimates by $0.09.

Going beyond the two headline numbers, we see Oatly’s made some good progress on the bottom-line front. Gross margins expanded by 5.4 percentage points in Q4 to 28.8%, on the back of supply chain efficiencies, particularly in the North American segment. Also, its adjusted EBITDA loss improved substantially from -$6.1 million to -$19.2 million in Q4. To top things off, its net loss during the quarter came in at $91.2 million, a remarkable improvement from the $298.7 million loss in the same period last year.

Furthermore, despite the sluggish category trends, Oatly was able to post almost 10% volume growth in Q4 and 9% for the full year on the back of healthy distribution gains. Also, the company is now looking to operate a more streamlined manufacturing setup, with a “fit‐for‐purpose” network that drives cost savings and better capacity usage. Perhaps the pièce de résistance was the company's announcement of achieving a full year of profitable growth in 2025, with expected positive adjusted EBITDA between $5 million and $15 million.

Though that may seem impressive on paper, plenty of question marks remain around the company's outlook, given the shaky long-term prospects. Firstly, its revenue numbers will be taking a hit this year, which Oatly specifically links to the loss of volume from its largest food service client in the U.S. Initially, it was eyeing 5% to 7% constant-currency growth this year but later slashed that forecast to 2% to 4%. Looking beyond the numbers, the loss of volume indicates a potential plateau or rebalancing in the company’s partnership with one of its key customers.

Furthermore, a big part of the company’s improved bottom-line numbers can be linked to impairment charges. In the previous year, it had large one-time write-downs linked to its Texas and U.K. facilities. In comparison, the total amount of those expenses came in substantially lower in Q4 2024, which were mostly linked to the closure of its Singapore and China projects. The lower impairment/exit costs explain almost two-thirds of its overall quarterly net loss improvement.

Moreover, despite the bottom-line improvements, its liquidity position remains mostly in shambles. Its cash‐to‐debt ratio of 0.2 suggests that for every dollar of debt, it has a measly 20 cents of cash. Also, its debt-to-equity ratio of 4.69 times underscores its heavy reliance on debt. Finally, its Altman Z‐Score of ‑1.95 sits firmly in the “distress” zone, pointing to severe bankruptcy risk. Taken together, these metrics highlight the company’s topsy-turvy financial health.

From a valuation standpoint, Oatly stock looks expensive relative to its modest growth outlook. It's trading at around six times its 2025 sales estimates, and more than 5.4 times 2027 estimates. Bear in mind, this isn't a hyper-growth company where such multiples can be justified, with consensus growth estimates falling in the 4% to 7% range. Hence, with modest top-line growth and a slight turn to positive EBITDA, which Charlie Munger famously called "bullsh*t" earnings, there’s not much to write home about.

Risks To The Thesis

The bulls would argue that Oatly has cemented its brand equity in the oat milk industry, and with its recent belt-tightening measures, it could potentially make a comeback. The recent profitability improvements add more fuel to the argument, which places it in a much more advantageous position than the past several quarters. Moreover, Oatly intends to tackle the negative publicity of oat milk, with plans to directly address “taste” misconceptions and “nutritional misinformation”. If it can effectively execute its marketing strategy, things could potentially move in the other direction. However, it remains to be seen how its plans will unfold at this stage.

Takeaway On Oatly Stock

Oatly stock has been taken to the cleaners, but mostly for the right reasons. The company lacks a significant moat in an industry facing decline, adding to the complexity. Hence, despite the hammering, it's best to avoid the stock before it shows meaningful progress in drumming up strong sales numbers.

data.title: Changing Our 5.5% Call For The U.S. 10Yr
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA

Doge, Bessent and SLR talk contribute to a taming of extent of likely Treasury yield rises ahead

We don’t make calls on politics. But politics absolutely impact our calls on rates. When Donald Trump was elected president of the United States it marked a seismic shift, versus the previous regime, and even relative to the first Trump one. Once the dust settled on the outcome, we posed the question (here) – “Can the US 10yr yield top 5.5%?”. We answered “yes”. Of course it still could, but we’ve decided to tame the call to 5% area, removing 5.5% as the official call for end-2025.

We’ve done that for a two main reasons, and one minor but important one.

  • First, the Department of Government Efficiency; the doge-tracker.com site has logged US$55bn of “tax dollars saved” so far, 2.75% of the US$2tn goal. While it is true that we knew about doge beforehand, what we did not anticipate was the intensity of its application. It remains unclear how realistic the doge spending cut goal is. But still, it can’t be ignored as something that could help contain the fiscal deficit, and by extension Treasury issuance requirements.
  • Second, we can’t ignore Treasury Secretary Bessent’s remarkable laser focus on the 10yr yield, and his suggestion that it’s this rate that the Trump administration would like to get lower. While Bessent cannot control the 10yr yield per se, it’s clear that he has an overt ambition to get it lower. All Treasury Secretaries would of course like to have as low a 10yr yield as possible, but few have been as vocal as Bessent on it. Leaving the funding profile front-end-heavy reflects the same.
  • Third, potential adjustments to the supplementary liquidity ratio, in particular with reference to inclusion of Treasuries in the measure. During the pandemic the Fed implemented a temporary rule allowing banks to deduct Treasuries from their SLR calculation. Although since reversed, recently Chair Powell has voiced an openness to making such an adjustment again, but this time more permanent. This would free up balance sheet at banks, ultimately adding to liquidity in US Treasuries. And that in turn helps Treasury yields to trade lower than they would otherwise.

Treasuries have been re-rated versus the risk free rate

Recent reversal lower in the 10yr swap spread symptomatic of an easing in excesses

We believe that these three factors have already been somewhat impactful already, as we see this on the spread from 10yr SOFR to the 10yr Treasury yield. At the end of 2024, this spread, had widening out to the 50bp area. That gap between the 10yr risk free rate and Treasuries was in part a reflection of supply pressure, and we were concerned that it could head out towards 75bp, mostly as there was no credible plan to solve the fiscal deficit issue.

US 10yr yield was becoming untethered, but less so now

That potential untethering of the 10yr yield was an important driver that could have pushed the 10yr yield towards 5.5%. Instead, the 10yr SOFR swap spread has re-tightened in the past few weeks, and is now at sub-40bp. Why is it tighter? For all of the reasons identified above – 1. Doge, 2. Bessent and 3. SLR adjustment talk. These act to reduce the negative credit perception towards Treasuries, while improving Treasury market liquidity, with some verbal suasion to boot.

An important additional point – the more successful doge is, the bigger are the impact risks posed to the wider macro economy. Getting government spending down is positive as it reduces the fiscal deficit, but lower government spending directly lowers GDP. Add to that a growing aggressiveness on tariffs. Tariffs pose near-term price rise risks (coming months and quarters), and in that sense are negative for US Treasuries, but the sting in the tail comes in the guise of risks to activity in the medium term (subsequent quarters and years).

That said, we're still structurally bearish on Treasuries. Now targetting the 5% area

That all being said, we remain structurally bearish on Treasuries for 2025. The US 10yr Treasury yield at around 4.5% is flat to our estimate of neutrality. It feels from here it’s likely to trade in a 50bp range around that – effectively 4.25% to 4.75%. And breaks above or below these extremes would result in practically inevitable tests of either one of 4% or 5%. Structurally, and as a call for 2025, we maintain the view that a trek towards 5% is most likely.

The Trump administration has stimulus at its core

The back story centres on US growth that remains lively, and the Trump administration has stimulus at its core at practically any cost. That, plus an already bloated US fiscal deficit, rationalises why the Fed will stop cutting at well above the 3% neutral area; we think some 75bp – 100bp above this. That should be echoed out the curve. Even if the 10yr yield is pulled lower initially, it will ultimately be prone to re-testing higher again. We think it will get back up to the 5% area.

This is not necessarily a call for the here and now. It’s a call for the coming number of months, and in fact it could be something more for later in the year. By that time, US tariffs will begin to actually show up on CPI readings, and tax cuts will finally be through Congress (although it remains to be seen whether material additions are made beyond the extensions). It’s always been a story of cumulative pressure that builds as we go through the year.

And don't forget the curve - it's far too flat, on spot and forwards

And finally, don't forget that the curve is remarkably flat, still. If the Fed ultimately lands in the 4% area, which is where the market mindset is, then we should have the build of a yield curve on top of that. The logic is once the Fed has bottomed, the next move should be up. Whether rates subsequently get hiked or not, the market will build a steeper curve, from the back end. That alone can get us to the 5% area on the 10yr.

What’s the risk to the view? That we gradually enter a recessionary tendency. And in fact, we do identify a growth recession risk for 2026. The thing is, between now and then (2025), we have a sticky inflation / elevated deficit combination front and centre that should remain as the dominant bully of the Treasury market.

And for all the reasons as laid out pre and post the US elections

This still fits with views we’ve been espousing since the middle of 2024. In May 2024 we laid out 4.5% as a neutral level for the 10yr, and that we should trade above neutrality. In July 2024 we doubled down on our call for 5% for 2025, and identified a 50bp delta for the US 10yr between a Harris outcome versus a Trump one. In August 2024 we homed in on a break above 5% as a point estimate for end 2025 on a Trump win. And in December 2024, we again went with 5%+ as a call the US 10yr yield. And we’re still on that. We’re just downsizing the extension to 5.5%.

data.title: TLT: Fear Vs. Reality
data.description:
The iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT) dropped a hefty -16.4% from the September high into a January low of $84.96 as bonds priced in higher inflation and growth, partially in anticipation of President Trump's proposed policies, and partially due to the Fed's rate cuts. Perhaps surprisingly, it bottomed the week before President Trump took office and has recovered over 4%. This article looks at what is behind the bounce and why it has the potential to develop into a trend.

Bracing for the Worst

TLT bottomed on January 14, the day after stocks bottomed (SPY) and the US dollar topped. This was partly due to cooler-than-feared CPI, but mostly because a lot of bad news had already been priced in and both TLT and SPY had fallen into significant technical support (SPY filled the gap left on election day).

No one knew at the time what was going to happen the following week when Trump took office. I was already long TLT and slightly anxious there would be an immediate trade war with Canada, Mexico, and China. I also feared Trump would pressure the Fed into rate cuts which could have the opposite of the desired effect on long-term yields - at least some yield rally since September can be attributed to the Fed's 100bps cuts in short-term rates and their effect on growth and inflation.

So I was bracing for a further drop, and I suspect many felt the same way. However, the news flow has not been as bad as feared, and four key areas may even be positive for TLT.

1. The Trade War

The trade war was the perhaps biggest unknown of Trump's policies, and to a large part, still is. There is still a major risk of tariffs when the Department of Commerce publishes its report in April. Additionally, exact details on reciprocal tariffs are still to be figured out. So far, though, the developments have been positive for TLT - not massively inflationary and a slight dent to growth.

There have only been blanket tariffs on steel and aluminum, and since steel was already at 25% since Trump's first term, it was mostly an increase in aluminum tariffs from 10% to 25%. The inflationary impact should be modest. Chinese tariffs have also been implemented, but there were fears they could be more aggressive than the 10% announced.

Trump has used the threat of tariffs to strong-arm Canada, Mexico, and Colombia into agreements over border policy and deportations. It's been an effective negotiating tactic and as long as countries yield to demands, tariffs may not be as aggressive as markets have priced in.

2. The Treasury

Scott Bessent was a welcome choice for Treasury Secretary. He was considered the market-friendly, steady-hand choice with market experience having founded hedge fund Key Square in 2015 and previously worked with George Soros and Stanley Druckenmiller. Alternatives were considered more radical and riskier and TLT closed over 2.5% higher when he was announced as Trump's pick. Again - and this is a point that keeps coming up - the worst-case scenario didn't happen.

The Treasury's Quarterly Refunding Announcement in February was Bessent's first major assignment. This had the potential to shake up the previous regime but went off with barely a ripple in bond markets. Borrowing was kept essentially the same as in Q4 under Janet Yellen.

During the January – March 2025 quarter, Treasury expects to borrow $815 billion in privately-held net marketable debt, assuming an end-of-March cash balance of $850 billion. [2],[3] The borrowing estimate is $9 billion lower than announced in October 2024, largely due to a higher beginning-of-quarter cash balance, partially offset by lower net cash flows.

Perhaps of more importance, Bessent focused the majority of the borrowing on short-term notes and bills rather than long-term bonds. This was a tactic used by Yellen to reduce the supply of bonds in an attempt to keep the lid on long-term yields. Bessent was critical of the approach but kept it unchanged as the below table shows. This should be positive for TLT.

3. Fed Friction

Friction between President Trump and the Federal Reserve was inevitable. Trump wants lower rates, while the Fed wants to keep them steady. Indeed, they may have already made a mistake by cutting 100bps as inflation and growth have increased, and the labour market is tightening again.

On January 23, Trump told the World Economic Forum in Davos.

With oil prices going down, I'll demand that interest rates drop immediately, and likewise, they should be dropping all over the world.

Long-term yields gained that day and TLT dropped 1.2%, which is probably not what Trump wanted to see.

Thankfully for TLT, the Fed has not immediately yielded to demands and maintained a stance of being in "no rush" to cut rates in its late January meeting.

In another positive, it appears Trump and the Treasury acknowledge inflation needs to moderate before the Fed can realistically cut again. Just ahead of the January meeting, Trump took to Truth Social to say:

Because Jay Powell and the Fed failed to stop the problem they created with Inflation, I will do it by unleashing American Energy production, slashing Regulation, rebalancing International Trade, and reigniting American Manufacturing.

Notably, he did not call for lower rates like he did in his prior post. The Treasury echoed the need for lower inflation in its February 3 Economy Statement for the Treasury Borrowing Advisory Committee.

Further moderation is needed before inflation is sustainably consistent with the Federal Reserve’s target.

This sounds restrained and reasonable, and in general, it seems like Trump, the Treasury, and the Fed can work together. That won't stop some outbursts, though. Just ahead of the February 12 CPI release, Trump again took to Truth Social and said:

Interest Rates should be lowered, something which would go hand in hand with upcoming Tariffs!!! Let's Rock and Roll, America!!!

Needless to say, bond markets did not like the double whammy of the CPI beat and the call for lower rates and TLT closed nearly 1.5% lower.

In a slight workaround to lowering rates, the Fed did make some dovish moves in its January meeting which were revealed in this week's release of FOMC minutes.

...regarding the potential for significant swings in reserves over coming months related to debt ceiling dynamics, various participants noted that it may be appropriate to consider pausing or slowing balance sheet runoff until the resolution of this event.

In other words, QT looks likely to slow. If the Fed were to also modify the duration profile, it could be a plus for TLT.

4. DOGE

DOGE, or the Department of Government Efficiency, has been making headlines for its aggressive approach. Markets may not have fully priced in the effect of DOGE’s actions as it was not completely clear how serious the whole operation would be. However, in the first month of Trump’s term, there have been large cuts and lay-offs, and it's already showing up in the data.

Whether this saves as much money as Elon Musk claims may not matter entirely. Consider that some growth in Biden's term came from government positions and just before the election the household survey showed that +785K jobs were in government positions, the second-largest rise on record.

Not only will there not be this growth, but those positions are being cut.

Higher unemployment should lead to lower growth, lower inflation, and lower yields. If DOGE can also lower the government deficit and issuance of debt, all the better for TLT.

Risks

One risk is that foreign countries have large holdings in USTs. Some countries are reducing their holdings and may do so more aggressively going forward. This added supply would weigh on TLT.

There is the threat of an escalation in the trade war in April.

TLT is volatile due to its duration and there is a risk of significant losses.

Conclusion

TLT has recovered slightly from the January low and has the potential to climb further. So far, President Trump's second term has not been the negative driver many feared. Indeed, some of his policies may be a positive for bonds going forward. I continue to hold.

Portfolio Review

Equities gained during the period. U.S. equities broadly advanced during the quarter, despite a pullback in December. The rally included mid-cap stocks after the overhang of the U.S. elections lifted in November. Inflation remained stubborn, and investors have been rethinking their assumptions about 2025, given that the Federal Reserve (Fed) is likely to enact fewer interest rate cuts than they previously anticipated.

Growth stocks outperformed. Growth stocks outpaced their value counterparts during the quarter, even as some investors sought lower-volatility positions. Consumer discretionary, communication services and financials advanced during the quarter, while real estate, health care and materials declined.

Consumer staples detracted. Consumer staples trailed the benchmark during the period, and the portfolio’s overweight position in the sector relative to the benchmark weighed on performance. Weak volumes in the beverages industry slowed performance for portfolio holdings like Heineken and Pernod Ricard.

Consumer discretionary. Security selection in the consumer discretionary sector weighed on results. Allocation among names in the automobile components industry detracted from relative returns, as did a lack of exposure to hotels, restaurants and leisure industry stocks.

Materials boosted performance. Investment decisions in the materials sector gave the portfolio a lift. A lack of exposure to the metals and mining industry, as well as limited holdings in the chemicals industry relative to the benchmark, aided performance.

As of 12/31/2024The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to purchase or sell a particular security. Equity holdings are grouped to include common shares, depository receipts, rights and warrants issued by the same company. Fund holdings subject to change.

Key Contributors

UnitedHealth Group (UNH). A lack of exposure to this health services and insurance company aided performance. UnitedHealth Group lowered guidance for 2024 during the period. We think increasing regulatory uncertainty related to pharmacy benefit managers, Medicare Advantage and managed care could cloud the company’s outlook.

Cisco Systems. This technology conglomerate outperformed after beating analysts’ expectations across the board. Investors were hopeful that Cisco’s improved margins were a sign that the company’s acquisition of Splunk was contributing meaningfully to revenues.

The Charles Schwab Corp (SCHW). Shares of this brokerage and wealth management firm gained during the quarter. Charles Schwab raised its revenue guidance and reduced its short-term debt, as clients have been keeping more uninvested cash in the company’s sweep accounts.

Key Detractors

Pernod Ricard (OTCPK:PDRDF)(OTCPK:PRNDY). This France-based maker of wines and spirits has underperformed. Like many of its peers in the beverage industry, Pernod Ricard has seen weak volumes, particularly in the U.S.

CVS Health (CVS). This health care company underperformed, in part due to regulatory uncertainty for pharmacy benefit managers under the forthcoming Trump administration. New legislation introduced in Congress seeks to ban common ownership of pharmacies and pharmacy benefit managers.

Medtronic. This medical device company has posted earnings roughly in line with analysts’ expectations, but our research indicated it has been affected negatively by currency impacts and weakness in its business in China. We also believe Medtronic has been hampered by uncertainty regarding health care policy under the forthcoming Trump administration.

Notable Trades

The Estee Lauder Cos (EL). We initiated a position in this beauty and cosmetics company after an extended period of underperformance resulted in what we viewed as an attractive entry point for its shares. We think its business can stabilize and its earnings can improve.

DENTSPLY SIRONA (XRAY). We initiated a position in this dental equipment manufacturer because we believed it offered an attractive risk/reward profile. DENTSPLY SIRONA’s profits have been weak as interest rates have been elevated and demand for the company’s high-end dental procedures has been soft.

Cie de Saint-Gobain (OTCPK:CODGF)(OTCPK:CODYY). We exited our position in this diversified building products manufacturer to fund other opportunities that we believed offered better risk/reward profiles.

Commerce Bancshares (CBSH). We exited our position in this bank holding company due to outperformance. In turn, we funded other investments that we believed were more attractive.

Portfolio Positioning

The portfolio seeks to invest in companies where we believe the valuation does not reflect the quality and normal earnings power of the company. Our process is based on individual security selection, but broad themes have emerged.

Value stocks may offer resilience. Investors continue to face macroeconomic and geopolitical risks, and even though the Fed has started cutting interest rates and inflation has moderated, we believe interest rates will remain elevated. Against this backdrop, we think value-oriented stocks should produce more predictable earnings and carry less valuation risk than their growth counterparts.

Opportunities in health care. Our research has led us to several health care stocks that we think offer attractive risk/reward profiles and possess compelling valuations. We consider health care stocks to be generally less prone to the cyclical effects of the economy because demand for their products and services tends not to be significantly impacted by the economy’s performance. In addition, we believe health care utilization rates will continue to normalize after the lagging effects of the COVID-19 pandemic kept patients from seeking medical procedures and devices.

Navigating the financials sector. We ended the period with a slight underweight to the financials sector relative to the benchmark. We remained overweight the banking sector but are underweight in insurance and consumer finance. We believe banks continue to face ongoing headwinds to earnings from rising deposit costs, increased regulatory requirements and normalizing lending conditions. Still, we see what we believe are good opportunities in the sector due to what we think are its relatively attractive valuations, moderating interest rates and a potential shift in sentiment if recently enacted regulations are rolled back. We continue to seek what we believe are attractively valued equities in the financials sector that have the potential to generate consistent returns from diverse sources, while also maintaining strong capital and liquidity ratios and lower risk.

We continue to see value in energy. We maintained above-benchmark exposure to diversified energy companies and equipment and services companies. We believed our holdings were generally higher quality, with solid assets, strong balance sheets and management teams focused on returns on capital. We think many companies in the energy sector have improved how they allocate capital by being more disciplined in capital spending and acquisitions while returning more capital to shareholders through share buybacks and dividends.

Underweight in industrials relative to the benchmark. While we have identified several stocks in the industrials sector that we believe offer attractive valuations, we remain underweight in the sector.

Portfolio Review

Stocks posted gains. The reelection of former President Donald Trump initially pleased investors who had worried about another protracted legal battle regarding the election’s results. However, the Federal Reserve’s signal that it will slow the pace of interest rate cuts amid persistent inflation largely erased postelection gains.

Growth outperformed at every capitalization range. Mid- and large-cap growth stocks led U.S. equities higher during the quarter, outperforming small-cap growth stocks, which posted more modest gains. Value stocks declined across the market-capitalization spectrum.

Information technology detracted. Stock selection in the sector weighed on performance. The semiconductors and semiconductor equipment industry detracted, largely due to our underweight allocation to Broadcom relative to the benchmark.

Biotechnology detracted. Stock selection in the industry was negative and helped drive sector underperformance. Regeneron Pharmaceuticals was a significant detractor.

Communication services was a top contributor. Stock choices in the interactive media and services industry helped sector outperformance. Google's parent company, Alphabet, was a solid contributor, buoyed by the launch of its quantum computing chip, Willow, and investor optimism about Alphabet's artificial intelligence focus.

Key Contributors

Microsoft. The software giant reported better-than-expected earnings, but the stock fell on investor concerns about Microsoft’s spending on artificial intelligence. We own Microsoft but hold a smaller position than the benchmark, and our underweight benefited relative performance.

DocuSign (DOCU). The provider of software for e-document signing outperformed on better-than- expected revenue and earnings. DocuSign's management also raised guidance on strong growth in billings and subscriptions.

Lululemon Athletica (LULU). The athletic apparel company's stock rose after management reported earnings that beat analysts’ expectations and offered strong full-year guidance. Our research indicated both sales and margins improved, as did inventory levels.

Key Detractors

Broadcom (AVGO). Our underweight in this semiconductor manufacturer relative to the benchmark hampered performance. We believe Broadcom is benefiting from the market’s optimism about artificial intelligence, which is boosting demand for its chips.

Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN). Although the biotechnology company reported revenue and earnings above analysts’ expectations, investors appeared to be concerned about disappointing sales of its higher-dose Eylea, which is used to treat age-related macular degeneration.

Wingstop (WING). The stock of this restaurant chain fell after it reported strong revenue but weaker-than-expected profits due to higher expenses, some of which were related to its expansion plans. Wingstop’s stock was strong over the past year and was subject to profit-taking.

Notable Trades

Broadcom. Broadcom is a diversified software and semiconductor technology company. We took a position in the stock for what we believed was its growing semiconductor business, which is benefiting from exposure to artificial intelligence (AI).

Vertiv Holdings (VRT). Vertiv manufactures digital infrastructure for data centers and communication networks. We think that, as a market leader in power and thermal solutions, Vertiv is benefiting from surging demand for the power and cooling needed to build out AI computing capacity.

The Trade Desk (TTD). The company is the largest independent demand-side platform on the internet, helping advertisers find and purchase digital advertising impressions. We believe it has a unique competitive position, has an excellent financial model and is led by a visionary founder/CEO.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). We think AMD is a leading semiconductor maker with excellent management. We bought the stock thinking it was the No. 2 player in AI. But unfortunately, the gap with NVIDIA has only continued to widen. We sold the position and put the proceeds into Broadcom, whose AI business is thriving, in our view.

Portfolio Positioning

Our process uses bottom-up analysis aimed at identifying large-cap companies that we believe are capable of producing attractive, sustainable earnings growth. We seek to reduce what our research has identified are unintended financial risks and align the portfolio with company-specific risks that we think will be rewarded over time. As a result of this approach, our sector and industry allocations reflect those areas of the market where we believe we are finding opportunities at a given time.

Focus on enduring growth amid near-term uncertainty. We think market volatility will likely remain high as investors contemplate U.S. policy uncertainty alongside geopolitical risks. While consensus 2025 corporate earnings forecasts have predicted solid growth, we believe valuations on large caps aren’t cheap. However, lower interest rates typically do cut financing costs and benefit growth stocks in the long term. As a result, we remain positive on growth stocks because we continue to identify companies that we believe are capable of producing strong, sustainable earnings growth driven by powerful secular trends. Examples include digital advertising and business transformation; artificial intelligence (AI), mobile and cloud computing; process automation; and electric vehicle adoption.

Today’s returns depend on tomorrow’s earnings. Corporate earnings are key because stock returns are often a function of earnings growth and the price investors are willing to pay for these earnings. The market is typically forward-looking, trading on expectations of future growth. For this reason, we expect investors will focus on full-year 2025 earnings estimates, which still look too high, in our opinion. Overall, we remain generally positive on growth stocks going forward, given falling interest rates and the increasing likelihood of an economic soft landing. Nevertheless, we believe extremely high 2025 earnings forecasts are a potential risk, particularly for companies that fail to meet those lofty expectations.

Productivity is central to profit growth. We see lasting challenges to productivity growth in the movement toward nationalism, deglobalization and demographic trends of social inequality and aging global populations. In general, as worker productivity is critical to corporate profit growth, we remain hopeful that AI and other technologies could help offset these productivity declines over time. Moreover, our research has indicated uncertainty remains high on several fronts, which we think helps explain today’s extreme market concentration and recent bouts of volatility. In addition to the economy and interest rates, wars and policy uncertainty introduce other sources of potential volatility, in our view. We believe progress on any or all of these fronts could lead to broader market participation.

Volatility presents opportunities, in our view. Business conditions typically vary from quarter to quarter and year to year, and stocks go up or down in the near term for any number of reasons. However, we believe companies with solid long-term growth prospects are better situated to ride out uncertainty relating to economic and earnings growth. As a result, we believe our portfolio investments have significant long-term growth opportunities. As ever, we remain vigilant in monitoring what we view as the financial progress and risks of our investments. We seek to utilize short-term volatility as an opportunity to add to positions when we see share prices disconnect from our assessment of their long-term valuation potential.

data.title: American Airlines: Only The Start
data.description:
American Airlines Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:AAL) proved with its Q4 results that the airline was left behind for no rational reason. The company has made vast improvements in the business model and remains poised to further cut debt levels via another $4 billion debt-reduction goal. My investment thesis is ultra-Bullish on the stock due to the cheap valuation and growth opportunities from the expanded loyalty program.

$2 EPS Is Just The Start

American Airlines faced plenty of headwinds in 2024 with the self-inflicted injuries from altered corporate travel policies and ongoing impacts from higher debt levels. Not to mention, the airline industry faced overcapacity issues in the domestic market that impacted profits before Q4.

Yet, the airline still produced $1.8 billion in adjusted pre-tax profits and an EPS of nearly $2 for 2024. American Airlines even produced $2.2 billion in free cash flow for the year after reporting strong Q4 results due to a $0.20 EPS beat to hit $0.86 for the holiday quarter.

Now, American Airlines can move forward with initiatives to expand the business and build on this EPS level. The airline already announced the expanded AAdvantage co-branded credit card deal with Citigroup (C) with an expectation to grow loyalty revenues to $10 billion via 10% annual growth after watching spending on the co-branded credit cards soar 9.5% in Q4.

The company continues to forecast a $1.5 billion boost to pre-tax income from the 2024 levels. Remember, American Airlines just completed a year when pre-tax income was only $1.8 billion. The loyalty income boost over a period of ~5 years will nearly double income alone.

For now, American Airlines guided to 2025 EPS of $1.70 to $2.70 per share, versus consensus estimates at $2.45. The market wasn't overwhelmed by the company guiding to low-end numbers actually below the 2024 levels, though the free cash flow guidance is to top $2 billion again, further helping to repay debt.

The airline hit an operating margin of 8.4% in Q4. At an annual rate of $57 billion in sales targeted for 2025, American Airlines would produce an operating income of $4.8 billion, far above the $2 EPS in 2024.

Surviving And Thriving

The airline ended 2024 with net debt at ~$23.5 billion, with $7.7 billion in cash, pushing the total debt level above $30 billion. Net interest expense was still $364 million during the December quarter, for a $1.3 billion annual profit hit for a company that only reported $1.8 billion in pre-tax profits.

The management team continues spending significant effort discussing total debt level reductions, but a lot of the amounts achieved over the last few years involved using excess cash to repay debt. The latest goal is to pay down total debt to $35 billion, which includes amounts assigned to leases and is unclear on how much of this involves reducing the cash balance from above $7 billion as part of the debt reduction.

American Airlines faces additional costs after signing new union agreements in 2024. The airline forecasts non-fuel costs up mid-single digits in 2025 with a move to more in line with inflation levels in 2026.

All of these factors contribute to the EPS trend bottoming out in mid-2024. The consensus analyst estimates now predict a $2.39 EPS in 2025 followed by a big jump to $3.07 in 2026 when the new Citi deal kicks into full effect and starts boosting profits.

The stock only trades at ~5x the 2026 EPS target now, while American Airlines is producing strong free cash flows. The airline will continue boosting profits from lower interest expenses and the co-branded Citi deal, while working to generally improve the operating margins of the business.

The recent tragic crash of the regional jet with the Army Black Hawk helicopter at the Regan airport provides an even clearer indication of how airlines have changed. American Airlines hardly saw a blip on the stock, despite seeing an aircraft crash, killing 60 passengers and 4 crew members.

In the past, any negative headline has sent the stock and the business reeling. American Airlines and the sector are far better at resolving problems and coming out better on the other side.

The stock is cheap based on current EPS targets, while American Airlines has a substantial opportunity to boost the numbers over time. The stock has up to 50% upside to trade at similar multiples to Delta Air Lines (DAL) and United Airlines (UAL) while both of those stocks are generally considered cheap in the transportation sector at just over 7x EPS targets.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that American Airlines again proved the airline sector is in a different position now with the strong Q4 results. The stock remains exceptionally cheap, with easy hurdles to boost EPS to both the higher 2026 levels and much higher.

Investors should use the recent dip into the $15s and a potential gap close at $15, as another solid entry point.

data.title: Past Week Dominated By Corporate Earnings Stories
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Past week dominated by good news and a few corporate earnings (0:25). Taiwan Semiconductor and Intel and Broadcom (3:30). Medtronic down after results; healthcare sector sentiment (5:00). Highlighting earnings next week - Nvidia, Berkshire Hathaway (9:00). Walmart's cautious outlook (11:40). Fed's favorite inflation gauge coming (13:15).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our director of news at Seeking Alpha, welcome back to Wall Street Breakfast. Great to have you.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here, Rena.

RS: So talk to us about the markets. February 21, here we are. What do you have top of mind, middle of mind, lower than that if you'd care to share?

BS: I think this past week was dominated by few corporate earnings stories. I don't know that there was a firm direction given to the market by macro.

I think we're still trying to feel out the Fed, feel out the administration, the Trump administration. So I think that, the back and forth of individual stocks was kind of the news for last week.

And in in general, the news was relatively good.

RS: What what made it good? What was the good news?

BS: Well, just putting out a few of the of the highlights. AppLovin (APP) had a brilliant earnings reports. It's benefiting from a focus shift to AI and e-commerce. It's a software platform that advertisers use to enhance the marketing in content. And so it's a good B2B play if you're trying to get the temperature of the market in general.

The the stock surged 24% on the earnings report and continued higher in the following session. It was off 11% on sort of profit taking. So there are some valuation worries, but in general, its results were a sign that AI is still filtering its way through company results.

And so if you're an investor who can pick the next company that's going to see the big AI boost, you can still get in pre-catalyst for some of these stocks.

And another one for last week that had a big move was Analog Devices (ADI). It had better inspected earnings and guidance. Its bookings were gradually improving and increased its share buyback program. And that had the stock up 18% over the past week.

RS: Any other names that you would add into the good news list?

BS: I think ADI gives us a good boost to the chip sector in general. So you saw Micron (MU) up 14% in the past week and Texas Instruments (TXN) up 9% in the past week.

NVIDIA (NVDA) was up as well. I'm sure we're gonna talk about that when we are looking into to next week, but looking into the chips, something that was an interesting story in itself was Intel (INTC).

It got a boost after Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) and Broadcom (AVGO) were reported to have interest in buying parts of Intel. So breaking it up, Intel has been a long running turnaround hope. So the company is looking to get off the mat. And the M&A news I think is an interesting test of the new administration and what its regulatory oversight will be in terms of approving or not approving deals.

This one has a a lot of complexities, especially with the overseas elements of Taiwan Semiconductor. I mean, there's no deal in place now, so it there's really nothing for the Trump administration to say, but I think it's a good litmus test.

I think if you're interested in this sector, I think watching the headlines surrounding Intel and possible M&A activity could be a good sign of the feeling for both players within the space about consolidation as AI becomes more important and then also what the administration is going to do.

RS: What's the public sentiment around the deal happening?

BS: I think it's still up in the air. Generally speaking, the Trump administration was viewed as pro business, lower regulation. But, obviously, one of his other tenants is a nationalist bent, a, protectionist bent with the tariffs and immigration policy.

So while it might look the other way in terms of general antitrust concerns in terms of deal making, the foreign aspect of parts of this deal might be problematic. That's why I think it's a a good test to see where sentiment actually lies within the administration.

RS: And then any other names to add to the good news list and anybody to include the bad news or not as good news list?

BS: I think Alibaba (BABA) is one to look at. It was only up 6% in the past week, got a mild boost after its earnings reports. It's up 7% post earnings, but that was after already running up significantly in anticipation to the earnings. It was actually up eight sessions in a row in the days running up to the earnings announcement and is up 67% since mid January.

The post earnings boost came with strong AI related revenue, and that's been the hope over the past month is that those results would fulfill the promise that the company would show a big boost in AI.

And so since that was fulfilled, there was no letdown after the huge run up ahead of the earnings.

I think that's a good sign again for the AI trade that there are still pockets where that has not been baked into the the stock price yet.

RS: Anything on more of the bad news side that you'd care to add?

BS: Just for balance, we might as well mention that there were a couple of downside earnings reports in the past week. Medtronic (MDT) was down, it fell 7% on its results.

It's basically flat over the last twelve months. It beat on its earnings and missed on revenue. Investors feel that the bid to high single digit growth rates that the company's been showing are disappointing. It's kind of sluggish growth. And honestly, the stock has been in the doldrums for a long time now.

It came down during the pandemic as people pushed off - it's a medical device company - and so as people pushed off some surgeries because the medical community was obviously focused on COVID, that cut into its revenue and it hasn't been able to recover yet.

It's basically the same levels now as it was in October of twenty twenty two. So the stock has had a hard time kind of coming back after that post COVID drop. So it's it's worth kind of looking at.

You and I had talked about in the past couple weeks with the pharmaceutical companies coming out, that there has been a long standing post COVID hangover in the the health care sector, generally speaking.

So I think that's something to watch going forward is when do these companies get into the next cycle of growth.

And then just as another company that was on the downside, Unilever (UL), which is a consumer goods company, was down after its earnings. And the larger thing to note there is just that one of the worries was commodity inflation.

So it's just a sign that inflation remains problematic for some of these consumer facing companies.

RS: What are you seeing in terms of public sentiment, analyst sentiment, Wall Street sentiment about the health care sector?

Are they looking for some catalyst to move the chains for health care stocks? Are GLP-1s not making enough of a dent in some of the players? Is it stock specific? What are your thoughts or what are your what are you seeing from the market there?

BS: Just using Medtronic as the jumping off point. Some of the commentary after those earnings came out was just there was no visibility of when the next catalyst for that stock was, where the next wave of large growth.

So the company might be executing well in terms of moving the products that it has, but it's just not clear where that next jump forward was gonna come from. And I think that's probably true of a lot of these companies.

We were talking before about how the COVID darlings, the pharmaceutical companies that got a big boost from COVID, that was a lot of investment that they put into those drugs. So they basically put their pipeline on hold to move over to COVID during that time. And so I think some of them have had a hard time getting back to diversifying their R&D in such a way that they can get the next big one.

And I do think that the weight loss drug boost is built into the stocks. There's a lot of competition in that area now. And so as new data comes out and new products hit on on that front, I think they're all running into a crowded market.

So I do think there's a look of what the next big - you don't wanna call it a fad or a trend, but for lack of a better word, the next big health care trend is gonna be.

Yeah. It will be interesting to see given how the GLP ones have pretty much taken over society. It'll be interesting to see what either what's Part of the problem is that, you know, they've already taken over.

Right? So where's where's the next grow? Like, you know and and everybody, you know, whatever the your sort of political feelings on it, everybody got the vaccine for COVID. Everybody was, you know, taking, the test for COVID.

And so, there was sort of full saturation for those kind of things. So, what's the drug that's or the the product that's going to kind of tap into the broadest possible public adoption?

Because obviously if you go through, healthcare companies, you're gonna find a few winners. You know, companies that that had an excellent, you know, an excellent test for a cancer drug or something like that.

I mean, it's pretty easy to to find big winners in the healthcare space. But when you're talking about the health care space in general, you kind of need those kind of, overarching themes that are going to drive the entire sector.

RS: So looking to next week, you mentioned some big names coming up. We have Nvidia coming up on Wednesday.

We had Raul Shah on Investing Experts this week talking about Hims and Hers Health (HIMS) and why he's bullish on the stock and looking forward to those earnings on Monday, which also has Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), a bunch of names reporting.

What are the ones that you think the market is going to be most reacting to or most interested in?

BS: Berkshire's always gonna make headlines because people are gonna look for Buffett's macro assessments. So it's not going to be the results of the company specifically, but more about what Warren Buffett sees generally for the market. So watch for headlines there.

I think in terms of the the healthcare conversation, I think HIMS is a good company to look at just in terms of being a low price direct to customer company, having a different business model than a Merck (MRK) or Pfizer (PFE).

Seeing how its results are going, especially as we talk about weight loss drugs, that's obviously a huge part of their business. So I think that that's definitely interesting.

Other than those names, NVIDIA obviously is the the big one coming out. I think if you're talking about the AI trade, that'll be a good litmus test of how that's going. Especially look for commentary from them about DeepSeek specifically and growing competition in the AI space generally.

Also, they might have something to say about trade concerns, both tariffs and restrictions of selling certain ships to certain countries. So there there might be questions asked in the earnings conference call about that. So that would be interesting to look at.

And then just in a totally different sector, Home Depot (HD) and Lowe's (LOW) are both reporting next week. Home Depot especially saw a lot of strength in the second half of last year, but has been choppy lately.

A lot of that is concerns about the housing market with interest rates going up and the the price of loans going up.

But this could go either way for Home Depot if housing stock is getting older because home building is slowing down and its home equity loans become more expensive so people can't pay to hire a contractor to come do their addition.

You might see more do it yourself upgrades in houses, which would benefit Home Depot. So I would look at their commentary both about the housing market in general and just how consumers are responding.

RS: And a stock we mentioned last episode that reported this week was Walmart (WMT), and we talked about maybe they're going to comment on the tariff concerns.

Do you have any insight or takeaways there or updates for listeners?

BS: Walmart was down after its earnings down about 6%. The outlook was cautious. So I think the worry there was that the consumer in general is starting to to slow spending.

You and I talked last week about how Walmart might benefit from people replacing some of their purchases with lower cost things. As they move down the cost spectrum that Walmart might be the beneficiary, where people land as they start start squeezing their budget.

However, if they're having trouble grabbing customers even in the high inflationary environment, that might be a sign that people stop spending in general.

So I think there's an eye on the consumer. The recent economic news has been relatively strong, Jobs report and so forth. Next week, the GDP report is coming out. That's back. We're looking at some revised data, for the previous quarter. So that's not gonna be any new information.

But I do think that Walmart can act as kind of an economic indicator in itself, and it shows a little bit of worry on investors' part and on corporate executives' part about how much the consumer is gonna hold it up.

RS: Anything else to say about the macro picture?

BS: No. Just that next week is also going to see the PCE indicator released. That's the Fed's favorite inflation gauge.

Inflation data from last week was much stronger than expected, kind of sparked a mini panic about the Fed, really pushed back expectations. That's come back a little, in the past week. People have moderated their expectations.

Still, the next meeting for the Fed is still expected to be no interest rate cut, and then the meeting after that in May also expected to be no cut. But now in the June/July framework, people are starting to think that the next cut might happen.

So that PCE indicator is going to give a good idea of, it's either gonna confirm the sort of hot inflation that we saw a week ago, or if it gives a different indication, it might move that expectation a little bit. So if you're looking at interest rates, that's the next big piece of data coming out.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
